results.question:
An 18 -year-old woman with a 5 -year history of epilepsy comes to the office with her parents for a follow -up examination. 
She has sustained three seizures during the past 6 months; all of her seizures originate from the temporal lobe. 
Anticonvulsant therapy has not decreased the frequency of her seizures. The patient and her parents ask about the potential 
benefits of partial temporal lobectomy. The physician performs a literature search and finds a p ublished study conducted by a 
large surgical center evalua ting outcomes of the operation. A total of 85 patients who underwent partial temporal lobectomy 
during a 3 -year period were observed for 15 years to determine the number and frequency of seizures sustained 
postoperatively. Subgroup analyses were conducted of patients who reported no seizures 1 and 2 years postoperatively. A 
Kaplan -Meier plot of long -term outcomes is shown; the P-values provided represent subgroups compared with all other study 
patien ts. Which of the following is the most appropriate conclus ion to draw from these data regarding seizure recurrence 
after the procedure?

options.A: Long-term seizure control is best predicted by a seizure-free status two years after the procedure.
options.B: Most instances of seizure relapse took place during the second to third year following the surgery.
options.C: Approximately one-third of patients had no seizures at the six-month postoperative point.
options.D: The subgroup analysis does not provide statistically significant evidence to support a conclusion.

results.correct_answer: Long-term seizure control is best predicted by a seizure-free status two years after the procedure.
results.predicted_answer: Long-term seizure control is best predicted by a seizure-free status two years after the procedure.

results.question:
A 2-week -old newbo rn with trisomy 18 diagnosed at birth is brought to the office by his parents for a follow -up visit. He is 
unable to suck properly and receives all his nutrition via nasogastric tube. The newborn was born at 37 weeks' gestation to a  
29-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1. Apgar scores were 4 at 1 minute and 8 at 5 minutes. The father is age 30 years. They 
have a 4 -year-old daughter who is healthy. The mother reports that her son has trouble breathing and often stops breathing 
for up to 20 se conds at a time. H e has had no fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. Length is 46 cm (18 in; 5th percentile), weight is 
2400 g (5 lb 5 oz; 5th percentile), and head circumference is 32 cm (13 in; 10th percentile). Physical examination shows 
dysmorphic features with a small jaw and small head, as noted at birth. The patient is listless and his breaths are shallow. 
During the examination, he has episodes of apnea lasting up to 15 seconds. His tone is increased and he has overlapping 
fingers and rocker bottom feet. Th ere is a 3/6 systo lic murmur heard loudest over the left sternal border. Which of the 
following is the most appropriate next step?

options.E: Consultation for in-home palliative support services

results.correct_answer: Consultation for in-home palliative support services
results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

I believe that hospital admission to undergo polysomnography is the most appropriate next step because the patient is experiencing episodes of apnea, which is a concerning symptom that requires further evaluation. Polysomnography is a diagnostic test that can help identify the underlying cause of the apnea, such as sleep apnea or other respiratory issues. Given the patient's complex medical condition and the presence of dysmorphic features, it is essential to rule out any underlying conditions that may be contributing to the apnea. Hospital admission will provide the necessary resources and monitoring to conduct the polysomnography test and address any potential complications.

results.question:
A 79 -year-old retired executive schedules an urgent office visit. When you see him, he says that he had difficulty walking 
upon getting out of bed this morning because of dizziness, which started last week and worsened last night. He has a 20 -year 
history of hypertension, for which he was treated, but for the past 10 years his blood pressure has been normal without 
treatment. At his most recent visit 3 weeks ago, his blood pressure was 150/90 mm Hg and he was instructed to adhere to a 
low-salt diet. He dran k a moderate amount of alcoholic beverages in the past and smoked about 16 cigarettes daily from age 
20 years to age 50  years. He has not smoked cigarettes during the past 29 years. He is taking no medication at present. All  his 
close relatives have lived to be at least in their 80s. This morning, pulse is 78/min and regular and blood pressure is 160/9 0 
mm Hg. Neurologic examination shows instability when the patient is turning and horizontal nystagmus with change in 
position. Funduscopic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Cerebellar dysfunction secondary to chronic alcohol consumption

results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

The patient's symptoms of dizziness, worsening over the past week, and instability when turning, along with horizontal nystagmus with change in position, are suggestive of a cardiac cause. The patient's history of hypertension, with a recent increase in blood pressure, and the presence of a regular pulse, make cardiac dysrhythmia a likely diagnosis. The absence of abnormalities on funduscopic examination and the patient's age make other options less likely.

results.question:
A 78 -year-old woman who r esides in a nursing care facility has had increasing confusion and drowsiness during the past 10 
days. The head nurse says, "The patient is usually alert, talkative, and outgoing, but during the past several days she has 
become increasingly confused and withdrawn. She drifts off to sleep even during activities that she enjoys." Vital signs have  
remained normal, and she has been eating well. There has been no change in her medication regimen, which includes 
pravastatin for hyperchole sterolemia, amlodipine fo r hypertension, a daily 81 -mg aspirin, and periodic sublingual 
nitroglycerin for stable angina pectoris. On interview, the patient says she does not have any symptoms. The patient identifi es 
as White. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 117/84 mm Hg. 
The patient appears to be drifting in and out of sleep during the physical examination, but she is arousable and not in distr ess. 
She is oriented only to person and place. Ther e is a 5 -cm ecchymosis wi thout hematoma on the right parietal scalp. Pupils are 
equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation. Ocular movements are intact. Optic discs are poorly visualized due to 
bilateral cataracts. The remainder of the physical examination, including neurologic examination, shows no abnormalities. 
Results of laboratory studies are shown:  
 
Serum    Blood    
 Urea nitrogen  15 mg/dL   Hemoglobin  13.0 g/dL  
 Creatinine  1.0 mg/dL   WBC  8000/mm3 
 Na+ 136 mEq/L   Platelet count  160,000/mm3 
 K+ 3.9 mEq/L  Urine    
 Cl− 95 mEq/L   Specific gravity  1.015 (N=1.003 –1.029)  
 HCO 3− 25 mEq/L   pH 6.5 (N=4.5 –7.8) 
 Glucose  98 m g/dL   Protein  Negative  
     Glucose  Negative  
     Occult blood  Negative  
     Leukocyte esterase  Negative  
     Nitrite  Negative  
 
Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the patient's worsening condition?

options.E: Viral infection of the brain parenchyma

results.response:
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

The patient's symptoms of increasing confusion, drowsiness, and disorientation, particularly in the context of a recent fall (suggested by the 5-cm ecchymosis without hematoma on the right parietal scalp), are highly suggestive of a subdural hematoma. This condition is a type of traumatic brain injury that can cause gradual worsening of cognitive function, including confusion, disorientation, and decreased level of consciousness. The fact that the patient's vital signs have remained normal and she has been eating well makes a severe acute illness or infection less likely. While the patient's medication regimen and laboratory studies do not provide a clear explanation for her symptoms, the presence of a subdural hematoma is a plausible explanation for her worsening condition. 

The other options are less likely:

A. Medication interaction: There is no clear indication of a medication interaction that would cause the patient's symptoms.

C. Subclinical hypothyroidism: While subclinical hypothyroidism can cause cognitive impairment, it would not typically cause the patient's acute worsening.

D. Major depressive disorder: While the patient's symptoms of confusion and disorientation could be mistaken for depression, the presence of a clear physical cause (the ecchymosis) makes this less likely.

E. Viral infection of the brain parenchyma: While a viral infection could cause encephalitis, the patient's symptoms and laboratory studies do not suggest this diagnosis.

results.question:
A 12 -year-old girl with a seizure disorder is brought to the emergency department 40 minutes after her aunt found her 
unconscious in her backyard. The aunt reports that the patient is visiting her for the summer. On arrival, the patient is awa ke 
but drowsy. She says she is embarrassed because she forgot to bring her anticonvulsant medication from home. She requests 
that you not call her mother "because she will yell and get angry." Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 74/min, 
respirations are 16/min, an d blood pressure is 100/70  mm Hg. Physical examination shows no focal neurologic deficits. The 
patient requests that she receive one dose of carbamazepine now and that she be given a prescription to fill at the nearby 
pharmacy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Provide a single dose of carbamazepine and write a prescription for the medication
options.B: Give her the requested dose now, but refrain from writing a new prescription for her.
options.C: Contact the patient's mother to obtain consent prior to initiating treatment
options.D: Decline to reach out to the mother and ask the aunt to provide consent by signing the necessary form.
options.E: Write a prescription for her medication but do not give her a dose in the emergency department.

results.correct_answer: Contact the patient's mother to obtain consent prior to initiating treatment
results.predicted_answer: Contact the patient's mother to obtain consent prior to initiating treatment

results.question:
A 63 -year-old man comes to the office because of a 10 -minute episode of right eye blindness 2  days ago. The episode 
resolved spontaneously and there was no associated eye pain. Medical history is remarkable for hypertension and type  2 
diabetes mellitus. Medications include hy drochlorothiazide, metoprolol, glipizide, simvastatin, and 81 -mg aspirin. He is 
183 cm (6  ft) tall and weighs 104  kg (229  lb); BMI is 31  kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination 
discloses pupils that are equal i n size and reactive to light. Visual fields are intact. Visual acuity is 20/20 in both eyes with 
corrective lenses. Cardiac examination discloses a regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs. Neurologic examination 
discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most a ppropriate diagnostic study at this time?

options.A: Fluorescein angiographic evaluation of the right eye
options.E: Further diagnostic workup is not warranted

results.response:
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

My reasoning is as follows: The patient presents with a transient episode of monocular blindness, which is a classic symptom of transient monocular blindness (TMB) or amaurosis fugax. TMB is often caused by emboli originating from the carotid arteries, which can be atherosclerotic plaques or thrombi. Given the patient's age, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus, he is at increased risk for carotid artery disease. Therefore, the most appropriate diagnostic study at this time is carotid duplex ultrasonography to evaluate for carotid artery stenosis or occlusion.

results.question:

A 39 -year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-year history of progressively worsening shortness of breath. She had

now becomes fatigued and short of b reath after walking two to three blocks. During the past 2 weeks, she has noted a rapid

Medical history is remarkable for Graves disease, which was diagnosed 4 years ago and treated with radioactive iodine. 
Current medications include levothyroxine, a daily multivitamin, and an oral contraceptive. She does not smoke cigarettes or 
drink alcoholic beverages. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 57 kg (125 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C 
(98.6°F), pulse is 120/min and irregular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/85 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to

S2. There is a grade 2/6 systolic murmur heard best over the second intercostal space. Examination of the lower extremities

A 53 -year-old man is admitted to the hospital for treatment of acute pancreatitis. Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F ), pulse is 
90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 160/90 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. There is 
tenderness and voluntary guarding in the epigastrium with hypoactive bowel sounds. Physical examination otherwise shows 
no abnormalities. Pneumatic compression devices are in place on both lower extremities. Results of laboratory studies show a 
leukocyte count of 18,700/mm3, serum amylase concentration of 540 U/L, and s erum lipase concentration of 500 U/L 
(N<200). Orders are writte n that the patient is to be given nothing by mouth; intravenous fluids are administered and he is 
given fentanyl intravenously for pain. On the third day in the hospital he is noted to be diaphoretic with labored breathing.  
Temperature now is 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse is 95/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. There 
are decreased breath sounds at the left lung base. Abdominal examination is unchanged. Which of the foll owing is the most 
appropriate next step in the evaluation of th ese new findings?

options.A: Obtain a radiographic image of the thorax

results.correct_answer: Obtain a radiographic image of the thorax
results.predicted_answer: Obtain a radiographic image of the thorax
results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

Rationale: The patient's presentation of diaphoresis, labored breathing, and decreased breath sounds at the left lung base suggests a possible pulmonary complication, such as pulmonary embolism or pneumonia. The most appropriate next step in the evaluation of these new findings is to obtain a radiographic image of the thorax, which would be a chest X-ray. This would allow for the assessment of the lungs and the detection of any potential abnormalities, such as a pulmonary embolism or pneumonia.

results.question:
A 24 -year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3 -month history of intermittent episodes of irritability and insomnia. 
She says, "I'll be fine for 2 to 3 weeks, but then I get so sensitive to anything my husband says. My mood will last for a fe w 
days, then I'm fine again." In addition to insomnia, she has associated headache, fati gue, and overeating during the same 
period. The patient married 3 months ago, and she says her husband has become frustrated with the unpredictability of her 
moods. She works as a phlebotomist. Medical history is unremarkable and she takes no prescribed medications. She does not 
smoke cigarettes or use other substances. She drinks one to two alcoholic beverages weekly. Her last menstrual period was 1 
week ago. BMI is 20  kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits and physical examination discloses no abnorm alities. At this 
time, specific additional history should be obtained regarding which of the following?

options.D: History of exposure to emotionally distressing events
options.F: Relationship of symptoms to menstrual cycle

results.correct_answer: Relationship of symptoms to menstrual cycle
results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

The patient's symptoms of irritability, insomnia, headache, fatigue, and overeating, which occur in episodes lasting a few days, followed by periods of normalcy, are suggestive of a mood disorder. The fact that these episodes are triggered by minor stressors and are associated with changes in appetite and sleep patterns, points towards a diagnosis of Bipolar II Disorder. The patient's recent marriage and the husband's frustration with her unpredictable moods may also be contributing to her stress levels.

Option A, dietary consumption of carbohydrates, is relevant because it can affect blood sugar levels and potentially trigger mood swings, which is a common feature of Bipolar II Disorder. This is a key aspect of the "carbohydrate craving" hypothesis, which suggests that individuals with bipolar disorder may experience increased cravings for carbohydrates, particularly during episodes of mania or hypomania.

While options B, C, D, E, and F may also be relevant in a broader context, they are not as directly related to the patient's symptoms and potential diagnosis as option A.

results.question:
A 55 -year-old businessman comes to the office for a preemployment examination. The patient's last visit to a physician's

patient  takes no medications. His father died of coronary artery disease at age 66  years. His 80 -year-old mother was once 
treated for "some type of thyroid disease." The patient has a sedentary lifestyle and says, "My energy level isn't what it us ed 
to be." He frequently eats at fast -food restaurants. BMI is 30  kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C  (98.6°F), pulse is 82/min, 
respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 142/92  mm Hg. Physical examination discloses obesity but no other 
abnormalities. Results of fasting lab oratory studies are shown:

results.question:
An 8 -year-old girl is brought to the emergency department via ambulance 1 hour after the onset of a generalized tonic -clonic 
seizure. Paramedics noted a pulse of 50/min  followed by a 30 -minute period of asystole. En route, the patient was intubated 
and oxygen therapy and an epinephrine inotropic drip were initiated. On arrival, the patient is unconscious. Temperature is 
36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 40/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure cannot be obtained. Oxygen saturation is 99% 
on an F IO2 of 1.0. Physical examination shows mildly dysmorphic facies. Pupils are sluggish in response to light. Lungs are 
clear to ausculta tion with ventilated sounds. Cardiac examin ation discloses a regular rhythm and sinus bradycardia. Abdomen 
is soft and nontender. Neurologic examination discloses paralysis. Distal pulses are 1+ and weak. Echocardiography shows 
global hypokinesis with lateral inferior wall and septal akinesis. Left ventricular function is 20%. The patient is admitted to 
the hospital. Four days later, there has been no improvement in her condition. Life support is withdrawn, and the patient die s. 
At autopsy, examination of the heart shows evidence of an acute myocar dial infarction. Which of the following is the most 
likely underlying cause of this patient's death?

options.A: Abnormality in the electrical conduction system of the heart
options.B: Disruption of an atherosclerotic plaque within a coronary artery
options.C: Elevated resistance to blood flow through the pulmonary vascular bed
options.D: Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve
options.E: Left ventricular hypertrophy with impaired systolic function

results.correct_answer: Abnormality in the electrical conduction system of the heart
results.predicted_answer: Disruption of an atherosclerotic plaque within a coronary artery

results.question:
A 2-month -old boy is brought to the office because of a 3 -day history of yellow -tinged eyes and skin. He was delivered at 41 
weeks' gestation. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. A t delivery, length was 54 cm (21.3 in; 90th percentile), 
weight was 4200 g (9 lb 4 oz; 90th percentile), and head circumference was 37 cm (14.6 in; 90th percentile). Apgar scores 
were 9 and 10 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. He has been formula -fed and feeds every 3 to 4 hours without spitting up or 
emesis. He has pale bo wel movements two to three times daily. He receives no medications. Current length is 60 cm (23.6 in; 
75th percentile), weight is 5.7 kg (12 lb 9 oz; 65th percentile), and head circumfere nce is 41 cm (16.1 in; 90th percentile). 
Temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 130/min and regular, respirations are 40/min, and blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg. 
Physical examination shows diffuse jaundice and conjunctival icterus. Cardiopulmonary examination discloses no 
abnormalities. The abdomen is soft, and there is m ild hepatomegaly. Results of serum studies are shown:  
 
ALT  265 U/L (N=12 –45) 
AST  280 U/L (N= 22 –63) 
Bilirubin, total  9.1 mg/dL (N<1.0)  
 Direct  6.6 mg/dL (N<0.3)  
Protein, total  6.6 g/dL (N=4.6 –7.9) 
Albumin  4.5 g/dL (N=1.9 –4.9) 
 
Abdominal ultrasonography shows a hypoplastic gallbladder and small common bile duct. Which of the following changes is 
most likely occurring in this patient's l iver at this time?

options.E: Accumulation of large lipid droplets within hepatocytes

results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

The patient's presentation of jaundice, elevated liver enzymes (ALT and AST), and a hypoplastic gallbladder and small common bile duct on ultrasonography suggests a diagnosis of biliary atresia. Biliary atresia is a condition characterized by the obstruction of bile flow due to the absence or abnormality of the bile ducts. In this condition, the liver undergoes a process called bile duct proliferation, where there is an attempt to form new bile ducts to compensate for the obstruction. This is the most likely change occurring in the patient's liver at this time.

A 24 -year-old primigravid woman comes to the clinic for her first prenatal visit. Her last menstrual period was 6 weeks ago. 
Medical history otherwise is unremarkable and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Family history is remarkable for 
gestational diabetes mellitus in her maternal aunt. The patient’s vital signs are  within normal limits. Physical examination 
discloses no abnormalities. The patient tells the physician that she would like to be screened for gestational diabetes melli tus 
and wants t o make sure the test will not yield a false -negative result. Several screening tests are available and each test’s 
performance is depicted in the graph shown. Based on these data, which of the following screening tests is the most 
appropriate choice for this patient?

options.F: The available information is insufficient to make a determination.

results.response:
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

I believe that Test B is the most appropriate choice for this patient because it has a high sensitivity (0.95) and a relatively high specificity (0.85), which means it has a good balance between detecting true positives and avoiding false positives. This is important for screening for gestational diabetes mellitus, where a false-negative result could lead to delayed diagnosis and treatment. While Test A has a higher specificity, its sensitivity is lower, which may lead to more false-negative results. Test C is not an option, and Test D is not mentioned in the options. The available information is sufficient to make a determination, so option F is not correct.

results.question:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the office by her father because of a 2 -day history of a painful mass over the right side of her 
neck that has been increasing in size. The father says she cries when her neck is touched. She also has a 1 -day history of a 
temperature of 38.1°C (100.6°F). The pain and fever have been well controlled with acetaminophen and ibuprofen. The 
patient has no history of serious illness and receives no other medications. Her maternal first cousin had non -Hodgkin 
lymphoma, and her paternal aunt had acute myelogenous leukemia. Examinati on and appropriate testing are done, and the 
patient is diagnosed with lymphadenitis. The physician informs the patient's father of the diagnosis and prescribes 
amoxicillin -clavulanate therapy. The patient's father questions the diagnosis and asks that his daughter be referred to a 
specialist. In addition to expressing empathy, which of the following is the most appropriate physician response?

options.A: What concerns do you have about this diagnosis that make you feel a specialist referral is necessary?
options.B: I understand your concern given the family's cancer history, but I am certain of this diagnosis and believe a specialist referral is unnecessary.
options.C: What specific concerns do you have?
options.D: Your daughter's condition does not require a specialist consultation, as her symptoms are expected to resolve after finishing the prescribed antibiotic treatment.
options.E: What leads you to believe that a specialist's evaluation is necessary?

results.predicted_answer: What concerns do you have about this diagnosis that make you feel a specialist referral is necessary?
results.response:
<answer> A </answer>
<belief> 0.85 </belief>

This response acknowledges the patient's father's concerns and shows empathy, while also encouraging him to express his specific worries and questions. This approach allows the physician to address the father's concerns and provide reassurance, while also maintaining a collaborative and patient-centered approach.

results.question:
A 68 -year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife 2 hours after he collapsed into a chair and lost 
consciousness for 30 seconds. The wife says that during the 10 minutes prior to collapsing, the patient had nausea and two 
episodes of vomiting. The patient's medical history is significant for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and coronary artery 
disease. Medications are metoprolo l, 81 -mg aspirin, and simvastatin. BMI is 28 kg/m2. The patient is awake and fully 
oriented. Temperature is 37.3°C ( 99.2°F), pulse is 104/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 163/94 mm Hg. 
Oxygen saturation is 95% on room air. Physical examination discloses drooping of the right side of the patient's face. Muscle  
strength is 3/5 in the left upper extremity and 4/5 in the left lower extremity; strength in the right extremities is normal.  Gait 
is ataxic. The patient has difficulty swallowing wh en trying to drink a cup of water. This patient's symptoms are most 
consistent with injury to the brain in the area supplied by which of the following cerebral arteries?
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

The patient's symptoms suggest a left hemispheric stroke, given the weakness in the left upper and lower extremities, ataxic gait, and difficulty swallowing (dysphagia). The drooping of the right side of the patient's face (facial weakness) is also consistent with a left hemispheric stroke. The middle cerebral artery (MCA) is the most likely culprit, as it supplies a large portion of the lateral surface of the brain, including the motor cortex, which controls the left upper and lower extremities. The MCA is also responsible for the facial motor nucleus, which controls the muscles of facial expression. The patient's symptoms are not consistent with an anterior cerebral artery (ACA) stroke, which would more likely cause weakness in the lower extremities. The vertebral artery, posterior cerebral artery, and basilar artery are not directly related to the patient's symptoms.

results.question:
A 3-month -old female infant is admitted to the intensive care unit via the emergency department in winter because of

the emergency department today, temperature was 37.1°C  (98.8°F), pulse was 124/min, respirations were 80/min, and blood 
pressure was 80/35 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation was 78% on room air; after administration of supplemental oxygen, oxygen 
saturation increased to 94%. The infant's pallor has not subsided, and she is in obvious respiratory distress. Physical 
examination discloses head bobbing, nasal fl aring, and subcostal retractions with each breath. Auscultation of the lungs 
discloses crepitant crackles throughout both lung fields with scattered expiratory wheezing. The remainder of the physical 
examination discloses no abnormalities. The infant is pre pared for intubation based on her clinical status. Results of

      Lymphocytes  80% 
     Platelet count  220,000/mm3

A 44 -year-old woman, who is a known patient, comes to the office for a Pap smear. She says she has felt fine but has noted a 
significant increase in the amount of bleeding with her menstrual periods. She says, "My periods are still regular but they 
now last 10  days instead of  6. It seems the flow is very h eavy for at least 5  of the 10  days. With periods like this, I am 
anxious for menopause." She also reports frequent fatigue and occasional insomnia, but she attributes these symptoms to 
having three teenage d children. She has not had dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, or vaginal discharge. Medical history is 
unremarkable. She does not use any medications and says that she does not like to take pills. Family history is positive for 
colonic polyps. The patient has been separated from her husband for 5  years. She is oc casionally sexually active and always 
uses condoms. Pelvic examination today discloses no abnormalities except for an enlarged uterus, approximately 8  weeks' 
gestational size, which is unchanged from her p revious examination. Ultrasonography 2  years ago disclosed a myomatous 
uterus. Cervical cytology and appropriate cultures are obtained, and complete blood count and serum lipid profile are 
ordered. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic study at this time?

options.B: Measurement of serum concentrations of follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone
options.E: Direct visualization of the uterine cavity with an endoscope

results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

Rationale: The patient's symptoms of heavy menstrual bleeding, fatigue, and insomnia are suggestive of anemia, which is a common 
consequence of heavy menstrual bleeding. The patient's enlarged uterus, which is unchanged from her previous examination, 
raises the suspicion of a uterine cause of her symptoms. The patient's history of myomatous uterus, which was discovered 
2  years ago, is a risk factor for uterine fibroids, which can cause heavy menstrual bleeding. The patient's age and symptoms 
are also consistent with a diagnosis of adenomyosis, which is a condition in which endometrial tissue grows into the myometrium 
of the uterus. A colposcope is a tool used to examine the cervix and vagina for abnormalities, and it would be the most 
appropriate diagnostic study at this time to rule out cervical or vaginal causes of her symptoms.

results.question:
A 58 -year-old man, who is reco vering in the hospital 2  days after an uncomplicated elective right total knee arthroplasty, 
suddenly develops left -sided chest pressure without radiation. The patient was able to ambulate with assistance earlier today. 
Medical history is significant for hypertension, type  2 diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia , and osteoarthritis. His routine 
medications are enalapril, metoprolol, metformin, and rosuvastatin; since admission his routine medications have been 
continued except for me tformin, which has been replac ed with sliding -scale insulin. Enoxaparin was added for deep venous 
thrombosis prophylaxis. The patient is diaphoretic. Temperature is 37.0°C  (98.6°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 
20/min, and blood pressure is 90/60  mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 92% on room air. Jugular venous pressure is 15  cm H 2O. 
Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses an S 3 and a grade  2/6 murmur heard best at the right second 
intercostal space with no radiation. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with 2 -mm ST -segme nt elevations in leads II, III, aVF, V 1, 
and V 2. Intravenous heparin, 325 -mg aspirin, and sublingual nitroglycerin are administered, after which the patient's systolic 
blood pressure immediately decreases to 72  mm Hg. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's 
symptoms?
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

* The patient's symptoms of left-sided chest pressure without radiation, diaphoresis, and tachycardia are suggestive of a cardiac event.
* The ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, aVF, V1, and V2, which is consistent with an inferior wall myocardial infarction (MI).
* However, the patient's symptoms and ECG changes are not typical for a classic inferior wall MI.
* The presence of a new S3 heart sound and a grade 2/6 murmur at the right second intercostal space suggests right ventricular dysfunction or strain.
* The administration of intravenous heparin, aspirin, and sublingual nitroglycerin led to a decrease in systolic blood pressure, which is consistent with a right ventricular MI causing a right ventricular infarction syndrome.
* The patient's symptoms and ECG changes are most consistent with a right ventricular MI, which is a complication of a pulmonary thromboembolism (C).

While I believe that option C is the most likely cause of the patient's symptoms, I have a 15% doubt that I might be wrong, which is why my belief value is 0.85.

results.question: A study is conducted to assess physician use of β -adrenergic blocking agents as treatment for heart failure. results.question:

A 72 -year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1 -year history of worsening shortness of breath and occasional chest 
tightness and nonproductive cough. Her symptoms have varied in intensity but she has had several periods of worsening 
symptoms during the past 6  months. Medical history is unremarkable and she takes no medications. She has smok ed 
1½ packs of cigarettes daily for the past 50  years; she does not drink alcoholic beverages. She works as a florist. BMI is 
22 kg/m2. Vital signs are within norm al limits. The patient is not in respiratory distress. Auscultation of the lungs discloses a 
prolonged expiratory phase. Cardiac examination discloses no abnormalities. There are no rashes or lesions. Results of 
spirometry done in the office are shown:  
 
  Baseline  Post Bronchodilator  
FEV 1 60% of predicted  65% of predicted  
FVC  82% of predicted  82% of predicted  
FEV 1/FVC ratio  0.55 0.62 
 
Chest x -ray is shown. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition?

options.A: Chronic bacterial infection involving the bronchi
options.B: Bronchospasm triggered by inhaled allergens in the environment
options.C: Alveolar wall destruction resulting in enlarged air spaces
options.D: Diffuse interstitial inflammation with fibrosis in a patchy distribution
options.E: Eosinophilic infiltration of the lung interstitium

results.correct_answer: Alveolar wall destruction resulting in enlarged air spaces
results.predicted_answer: Alveolar wall destruction resulting in enlarged air spaces
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

The patient's symptoms of worsening shortness of breath, chest tightness, and nonproductive cough, along with a prolonged expiratory phase on auscultation, suggest a chronic respiratory condition. The spirometry results show a reduced FEV1 (60% of predicted) with a relatively preserved FVC (82% of predicted), indicating an obstructive pattern. The FEV1/FVC ratio improves with bronchodilator administration, which is consistent with a reversible obstructive process.

The patient's history of long-term smoking (1½ packs of cigarettes daily for 50 years) is a significant risk factor for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The chest x-ray findings are not provided, but the clinical presentation and spirometry results are consistent with COPD.

Option C, alveolar wall destruction resulting in enlarged air spaces, is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition, as it is a hallmark of emphysema, a subtype of COPD.

results.question:
A 77 -year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 2 -week history of gangrene of the right foot. Three years ago, he 
underwent a left transmetatarsal amputation. During hospitalization, he required intubation and mechanical ventilation for 2 
weeks because of sepsis. He then spent 2 months in an acute rehabilitation facility. Medical history also is remarkable for 
type 2 diabe tes mellitus, peripheral vascular disease, and hypertension. His medications are insulin, irbesartan, 
hydrochlorothiazide, and  aspirin. He is widowed, lives alone, and keeps in contact with his two sons. He has a living will, 
and his younger son is designated health care proxy. The patient is alert and fully oriented. Temperature is 38.4°C  (101.1°F), 
pulse is 90/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 156/90 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 96% on 
room air. There is a 5 × 6 -cm ulcer with black margins over the plantar surface of the right foot; malodorous, white -green 
exudate; and visible bone in the center of t he ulcer. Popliteal fossa pulses are decreased and posterior tibial pulses are absent 
bilaterally. Antibiotic therapy is begun. The patient is informed that he requires amputation of the right leg below the knee . 
He declines amputation, saying, "I don't ever want to go through what I went through before with my left foot." He is told 
that the potential risks for not proceeding with a mputation are loss of the entire right lower extremity and death. He still 
refuses the procedure. Which of the following is th e most appropriate action for this physician to take?

options.B: Seek guidance from the institutional ethics committee
options.C: Seek authorization for the surgical procedure from both of the patient's sons
options.D: Seek judicial authorization for surgical amputation
options.E: Seek authorization for below-knee amputation from the designated health care proxy (younger son)
options.F: Consult the patient's advance directive for further guidance

results.question:
A 63 -year-old woman is ev aluated in the hospital 2 days after she was admitted for management of left sternal chest pain and 
a hypertensive emergency with a blood pressure of 195/110 mm Hg. Workup included CT angiography of the chest, which 
showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism. During the past 6 hours, she has had decreased urine output. A bladder catheter 
yielded only 50 mL of urine during the past 3 hours. She has not had abdominal pain. Medical history is remarkable for 
hypertension, osteoarthritis, and stage 3 chronic kidne y disease with a baseline serum creatinine concentration of 1.7 mg/dL. 
Routine medications are lisinopril, amlodipine, and hydrochlorothiazide. The chest pain she had on admission has resolved. 
Temperature is 37.6°C (99.7°F), pulse is 87/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 147/87 mm Hg. Physical 
examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies obtained on admission and today are shown:  
 
  On Admission  Today  
Serum      
 Na+ 138 mEq/L  141 mEq/L  
 K+ 4.2 mEq/L  5.1 mEq/L  
 Cl− 119 mEq/L  118 mEq/L  
 HCO 3− 23 mEq/L  21 mEq/L  
 Urea nitrogen  26 mg/dL  26 mg/dL  
 Creatinine  1.6 mg/dL  2.4 mg/dL  
 Glucose  106 mg/dL  108 mg/dL  
Urine      
 Specific gravity  – 1.010 (N=1.003 –1.029)  
 Protein  – Negative  
 Blood  – Negative  
 Leukocyte esterase  – Negative  
 WBCs  – 0–3/hpf  
 RBCs  – 0–3/hpf  
 Casts  – Muddy brown  
 
Which of the following factors in this patient's history most increased her risk for developing her current complication?

options.D: Administration of an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor

results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

The patient's decreased urine output and elevated serum creatinine concentration suggest acute kidney injury (AKI). The presence of muddy brown casts in the urine is a specific finding associated with AKI, particularly in the context of rhabdomyolysis or myoglobinuria. The patient's history of chest pain and hypertensive emergency, along with the resolution of chest pain and improvement in blood pressure, raises the suspicion of rhabdomyolysis. The administration of a bladder catheter (option A) is the most likely factor that increased the patient's risk for developing her current complication, which is AKI due to rhabdomyolysis. The catheter may have caused trauma to the urinary tract, leading to the release of myoglobin from damaged muscle cells into the bloodstream and subsequently into the urine, resulting in AKI.

results.question:
A randomized placebo -controlled trial is conducted to assess the efficacy of duloxetine in patients with severe rheumatoid 
arthritis who are currently treated with methotrexate. Patients who have six or more joints that are actively inflamed, swoll en, 
or tender to palpation are eligible for inclusion in the study. The primary outcome measure is the number of joints that are no 
longer inflamed after 4  months of treatment. One hundred fifty patients are enrolled and randomized to the duloxetine group 
(n=75) or the placebo group (n=75). The mean number of involve d joints is 12. Data show that patients in the intervention 
group have a mean of seven joints that are no longer inflamed at the end of the trial; patients in the placebo group have a 
mean of two joints that are no longer inflamed at the end of the trial. Results of a two -tailed  t test show P=.02. Which of the 
following is the most  appropriate conclusion to draw regarding the clinical and statistical significance of this study?  
 
 Clinically Significant  Statistically Significant

options.A: The findings are not considered meaningful for patient care and do not represent a true effect of the treatment.
options.B: The observed effect is not considered meaningful in clinical practice, but the difference between groups is unlikely to be due to chance.
options.C: The findings demonstrate clinical importance but do not reach statistical significance.
options.D: The study demonstrates both a clinically meaningful effect and statistical significance.

results.correct_answer: The study demonstrates both a clinically meaningful effect and statistical significance.
results.predicted_answer: The findings demonstrate clinical importance but do not reach statistical significance.
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

Reasoning: The study demonstrates a statistically significant effect of duloxetine compared to placebo (P = 0.02), but the clinical significance of the effect is questionable. The mean number of joints that are no longer inflamed in the intervention group is only 5 more than in the placebo group (7 - 2 = 5), which may not be considered clinically meaningful in the context of severe rheumatoid arthritis. Therefore, the correct answer is C, as it acknowledges the statistical significance of the effect but questions its clinical importance.

results.question:
A physician would like to evaluate the benefit of a new osteoporosis medication in preventing fractures in women aged 
40 to 60 years. A total of 300  women aged 40 to 60  years with osteoporosis documented by DEXA scan are enrolled in the 
study from general gynecology clinics. The patients are equally randomiz ed into two groups. Group  A receives the new 
osteoporosis medication and Group  B receives placebo. The patients are followed for 5  years and bone density is measured 
annually by DEXA scanning. Results show that the bone density of patients who received the study medication is unchanged 
and the bone density in patients who received placebo decreased by 2% ( P=.01). The ph ysician concludes that the new 
medication decreases the risk for fracture in women aged 40 to 60  years. Which of the following is the most a ppropriate 
conclusion to draw from these data?

options.A: The effect of the drug on bone mineral density may not translate to a reduction in fracture risk.
options.B: The absence of bone density change in the treatment group implies the potential influence of a confounding factor
options.C: The medication's complexity may be inferred from the absence of bone density improvement in the treatment group.
options.D: The absence of female participants under the age of 40 limits the assessment of treatment efficacy over extended periods.
options.E: The inclusion of participants exclusively from gynecology clinics introduces a potential for selection bias.

results.correct_answer: The effect of the drug on bone mineral density may not translate to a reduction in fracture risk.
results.predicted_answer: The effect of the drug on bone mineral density may not translate to a reduction in fracture risk.
results.response:
<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

The correct answer is A because the study only shows an increase in bone density in the treatment group compared to the placebo group, but it does not directly measure the effect of the medication on fracture risk. The physician's conclusion that the medication decreases the risk for fracture is an assumption that is not supported by the data. The study only shows a change in bone density, which may not necessarily translate to a reduction in fracture risk. 

The other options are incorrect because:

* B is incorrect because the absence of bone density change in the treatment group does not necessarily imply the presence of a confounding factor.
* C is incorrect because the complexity of the medication is not relevant to the conclusion drawn from the study.
* D is incorrect because the study only includes women aged 40-60, and the absence of female participants under the age of 40 is not a limitation of the study.
* E is incorrect because the inclusion of participants exclusively from gynecology clinics may introduce a potential for selection bias, but this is not the most appropriate conclusion to draw from the data.

results.question: A 15 -year-old boy is brought to the clinic after his father noticed significant swelling of the boy’s chest when he was getting dressed 6  hours ago. The patient reports a 3 -month history of gradually worsening left -sided chest pain and swelling, but he says he was too embarrassed to tell his parents. He has not had fever, chills, night sweats, loss of appetite, weight loss, or any recent trauma to the chest. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. The patient is a good student. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. Temperature is 36.4°C (97.5°F), pulse is 69/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air. Examination of the head, neck, and skin discloses no abnormalities. There is a fixed palpable mass measuring approximately 14 × 12 cm in the left chest wall that is tender to palpation. The remainde r of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. results.question:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 2 -kg (5 -lb) weight loss, decreased appetite, and daily 
fatigue during the past 2 months . The mother says the patient's fatigue has limited his physical activity. The patient's medical 
history is remarkable for asthma and seasonal allergies. He has had three asthma exacerbations during the past 2 months that 
have required treatment with oral prednisone. Routine medications are cetirizine, albuterol, and budesonide. The patient is 9 9 
cm (3 f t 3 in; 25th percentile) tall and weighs 14 kg (31 lb; 25th percentile); BMI is 14.3 kg/m2 (10th percentile). 
Temperature is 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 70/mi n, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. The patient 
appears lethargic. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:  
 
Serum    Blood    
 Creatinine  0.8 mg/dL (N=0.03 –0.59)   Hemoglobin  12.0 g /dL (N=11.5 –14.5)  
 Na+ 125 mEq/L   WBC  10,000/mm3 
 K+ 4.5 mEq/L   Platelet count  250,000/mm3 
 Cl− 90 mEq/L  Urine    
 HCO 3− 25 mEq/L   Specific gravity  1.025 (N=1.003 –1.029)  
 Glucose  60 mg/dL (N=60 –100)  Protein  Negative  
     Blood  Negative  
     Leukocyt e esterase  Negative  
     Nitrite  Negative  
 
Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's current condition?
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

The patient presents with weight loss, decreased appetite, fatigue, and lethargy, which are non-specific symptoms that can be seen in various conditions. However, the key findings in this case are the patient's short stature (25th percentile) and low BMI (10th percentile), which suggest growth retardation. The laboratory results show a normal hemoglobin level, which makes anemia an unlikely cause of the patient's symptoms.

The patient's history of asthma and seasonal allergies, as well as the use of oral prednisone for asthma exacerbations, is relevant. Long-term use of prednisone can lead to Cushing's syndrome, which is characterized by excess cortisol production. However, the patient's laboratory results do not show the typical findings of Cushing's syndrome, such as hyperglycemia, hypertension, or hypokalemia.

The patient's low sodium level (125 mEq/L) is a key finding. The normal specific gravity of the urine suggests that the patient is not experiencing dehydration, which makes the low sodium level more likely to be due to a hormonal cause rather than a renal cause.

Considering the patient's symptoms and laboratory results, the most likely underlying cause of this patient's current condition is insufficient secretion of vasopressin (ADH), which leads to central diabetes insipidus. This condition is characterized by polyuria, polydipsia, and dehydration, but the patient's specific gravity is normal, and the low sodium level is more suggestive of a hormonal cause. The patient's short stature and growth retardation are also consistent with central diabetes insipidus.

The other options are less likely:

A. Impaired function of the 21-hydroxylase enzyme is a cause of congenital adrenal hyperplasia, which would lead to an excess of cortisol and other glucocorticoids, but the patient's laboratory results do not support this diagnosis.

B. Insufficient secretion of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) would lead to primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease), but the patient's laboratory results do not show the typical findings of Addison's disease, such as hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, and hyperpigmentation.

D. Excessive secretion of aldosterone would lead to Conn's syndrome (primary aldosteronism), but the patient's laboratory results do not show the typical findings of Conn's syndrome, such as hypertension, hypokalemia, and metabolic alkalosis.

E. Excessive secretion of insulin would lead to hypoglycemia, but the patient's glucose level is normal, and the patient's symptoms do not suggest hypoglycemia.

results.question:
A 38-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 4 -hour history of confusion and lethargy. Medical 
history is remarkable for major depressive disorder and a 6 -month history of moderate low back pain that began after she 
lifted heavy boxes during a move. Her medications are fluoxetine daily and acetaminophen with codeine as needed. Family 
history is remarkable for hype rtension and congestive heart failure in her mother. The patient lives with her husband and her 
mother. The patient dri nks two glasses of wine on weekends. On arrival, she is somnolent but arousable to sternal rub. 
Temperature is 36.4°C (97.5°F), pulse is 32/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 74/36 mm Hg. Oxygen 
saturation is 100% on room air. Pupils measure 6 mm in diameter. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Results of 
laboratory studies are shown:  
 
Serum    Blood    
 ALT  180 U/L   Hemoglobin  12 g/dL  
 AST  150 U/L   WBC  12,000/mm3 
 Creatinine  2.5 mg/dL   Platelet count  400,000/mm3 
 Acetaminophen  negative      
Urine toxicology        
 Opiates  positive      
 
An overdose of a drug with which of the following mechanisms of  action is the most likely cause of this patient’s condition?

options.E: Serotonin reuptake blockade in central nervous system neurons
options.G: Blockade of sodium and chloride reabsorption in the kidney

results.response:
<answer> A </answer>
<belief> 0.95 </belief>

The patient's symptoms, including somnolence, lethargy, and bradypnea, are consistent with opioid overdose. The positive urine toxicology for opiates further supports this diagnosis. Activation of mu-opioid receptors is the primary mechanism of action of opioids, leading to analgesia, sedation, and respiratory depression. The patient's presentation is most consistent with an opioid overdose, making option A the most likely cause of her condition.

results.question:
A 56 -year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-week history of fever and moderate pain and swelling of the left side 
of her neck. Medical history is remarkable for a 4 -year history of HIV infection. She has declined antiretroviral therapy and 
takes no medications. BMI is 20 kg/m2. Temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F); other vital signs, including oxygen saturation on 
room air, are within normal limits. Physical examination discloses warmth and moderate tenderness over the swelling shown 
in the photograph. Lungs are clear to auscultation. There is no rash o r hepatosplenomegaly. CD4+ T -lymphocyte count is 
160/mm3, and plasma HIV viral load is 56,000 copies/mL. Pathologic examination of a biopsy specimen of a cervical lymph 
node shows 2+ acid -fast bacilli, and result of a nucleic acid amplification test is positive for a drug -sensitive mycobacterium. 
The infection in this patient mo st likely reached the cervical lymph nodes through which of the following routes?

options.B: Contiguous spread from mediastinal lymph node involvement
options.C: Spread via the bloodstream from a pulmonary focus
options.D: Spread via lymphatic channels originating from the pharynx

results.correct_answer: Spread via the bloodstream from a pulmonary focus
results.predicted_answer: Spread via the bloodstream from a pulmonary focus
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief> 

The patient's presentation of fever, pain, and swelling of the left side of her neck, along with a history of HIV infection and a CD4+ T-lymphocyte count of 160/mm3, suggests an opportunistic infection. The presence of acid-fast bacilli in a cervical lymph node biopsy and a positive nucleic acid amplification test for a drug-sensitive mycobacterium are indicative of tuberculosis (TB). TB is a common opportunistic infection in HIV-infected individuals, particularly when the CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is low.

Given the patient's symptoms and the location of the infection, the most likely route of entry for the TB infection is through the bloodstream from a pulmonary focus. This is because TB often starts in the lungs and can disseminate to other parts of the body, including the lymph nodes, through the bloodstream.

The other options are less likely:

A. Direct cutaneous entry is unlikely because TB typically enters the body through inhalation of aerosolized droplets containing the bacteria, not through skin contact.

B. Contiguous spread from mediastinal lymph node involvement is possible but less likely given the patient's symptoms and the location of the infection.

D. Spread via lymphatic channels originating from the pharynx is possible but less likely given the patient's symptoms and the location of the infection.

E. Migration of infected lymphocytes is not a typical route of entry for TB infection.

Therefore, I believe option C is the most likely answer with a confidence level of 0.85.

My reasoning is as follows:

* The patient has a history of COPD, which is a chronic condition that can lead to acute exacerbations.
* The patient's symptoms, such as shortness of breath at rest, are consistent with an acute exacerbation of COPD.
* The patient's physical examination findings, including tachypnea (24/min) and retraction of the bilateral sternocleidomastoid muscles and intercostal muscles, are also consistent with an acute exacerbation of COPD.

Overall, the combination of the patient's history, physical examination findings, and laboratory results make an acute exacerbation of COPD the most likely diagnosis.

medical record from her visit 1  year ago shows that h er medications included clonazepam, clonidine, and lithium carbonate. 
Doses of each medication are also listed. Attempts to contact the patient's primary care physician and psychiatrist for more 
detailed medication history have been unsuccessful. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial approach to 
managing the patient's medications?

options.B: Call the patient's pharmacy to verify her active prescription list
options.C: Monitor the patient closely while continuing attempts to contact her psychiatrist
options.D: Prescribe the same drug regimen documented in her prior emergency department visit record

The most straightforward way to obtain a clear picture of her current medication regimen is to contact her pharmacy, which would have a record of her active prescriptions. This approach is non-invasive, efficient, and does not require access to her brother's residence or the patient's psychiatrist.

A 73 -year-old man is admitted to a nursing care facility from the hospital following a prolonged hospitalization for 
pneumonia, septicemia, and respiratory failure. Medical history is remarkable for advanced dementia, Alzheimer type, 
hypertension, and type  2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications include lisinopril, enteric -coated aspirin, and insulin 
glargine. The patient appears frail and is confined to bed. He opens his eyes to voice but is noncommunicative. BMI is 
27 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.2°C  (99.0°F), pulse is 66/min, respirations are 15/min, and  blood pressure is 142/86  mm Hg. 
Oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. Physical examination discloses the finding on the patient's right foot as shown in the 
photograph. The remainder of the examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely 
underlying cause of this patient's condition?

options.A: Infection caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
options.B: Bed rest for an extended duration
options.C: Necrotizing ulceration due to neutrophilic dermatosis
options.D: Atheroembolic disease originating from a proximal arterial source
options.E: Venous stasis resulting from incompetent valves

results.correct_answer: Bed rest for an extended duration
results.predicted_answer: Necrotizing ulceration due to neutrophilic dermatosis
results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

* The patient has a history of prolonged hospitalization, which increases the risk of hospital-acquired infections, including those caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). However, the patient's temperature is only slightly elevated, and there is no mention of other signs of infection, making option A less likely.
* Bed rest for an extended duration (option B) can lead to pressure ulcers, but the description of the patient's condition does not match the typical presentation of a pressure ulcer.
* Necrotizing ulceration due to neutrophilic dermatosis (option C) is a possibility, but it is less common and typically presents with other systemic symptoms.
* Atheroembolic disease originating from a proximal arterial source (option D) is a strong possibility, given the patient's history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, which increase the risk of atherosclerosis. The patient's presentation of a necrotic ulcer on the foot, which is a common site for atheroembolic disease, makes this option the most likely.
* Venous stasis resulting from incompetent valves (option E) is less likely, as the patient's oxygen saturation is normal, and there is no mention of other signs of venous insufficiency.

Overall, while there are some possibilities, I believe option D is the most likely underlying cause of the patient's condition, with a confidence level of 0.8.

results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

My belief in this answer is 0.8 because the patient's symptoms and physical examination findings are highly suggestive of intermittent claudication due to peripheral artery disease (PAD). The patient's ankle-brachial index (ABI) of 0.6 on the right is consistent with PAD, and the presence of femoral bruits and decreased pedal pulses further supports this diagnosis. The most appropriate pharmacotherapy for PAD is a medication that improves nitric oxide production, which is a potent vasodilator and can help to improve blood flow to the affected limb.

morning stiffness that usually resolves within 15 to 20 minutes. Recently  the joint pain has occurred at night and has 
restricted her activity. Ibuprofen has provided moderate relief of her symptoms. Medical history is remarkable for type 2 
diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity. Additional medications include glipizide, m etformi n, and hydrochlorothiazide. 
She is 168  cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 105  kg (230 lb); BMI is 37  kg/m2. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 70/min, 
respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 140/90  mm Hg. Examination of the hands shows bon y enlargements of the 
distal and proximal interphalangeal joints with mild tenderness to the interphalangeal joints. Examination of both knees 
shows bony enlargement with tenderness at the joint margin, limited range of motion, and mild joint effusions. Res ults of  
laboratory studies are shown:  
 
Serum    Blood    
 Rheumatoid factor  Nonreactive   WBC  8000/mm3 
 Uric acid  6 mg/dL      
 
X-rays of the knees are most likely to show which of the following?

options.A: Joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation
options.B: Joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation
options.C: Preservation of joint space without erosive changes or osteophyte formation
options.D: Asymmetric narrowing of joint spaces and sclerosis beneath the articular cartilage

<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.95</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

The patient presents with gradual onset of pain and swelling in both knees and distal interphalangeal joints of the hands, which is exacerbated by movement and accompanied by morning stiffness that resolves within 15-20 minutes. These symptoms are classic for osteoarthritis (OA), particularly given the involvement of the distal interphalangeal joints and the presence of bony enlargements and osteophytes on examination.

The laboratory studies show a nonreactive rheumatoid factor, which makes rheumatoid arthritis (RA) less likely. The patient's age, sex, and symptoms also make RA less likely.

The X-rays of the knees are most likely to show joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation, which are characteristic findings in OA. This is because OA is a degenerative joint disease that leads to the breakdown of cartilage and the formation of bone spurs (osteophytes) in response to the joint damage.

Therefore, I believe that option B is the correct answer, with a confidence level of 0.95.

results.question:
A 68 -year-old woman was admitted to the intensive care unit 36 hours ago after becoming obtunded due to respiratory 
distress. She is now intubated and chest x -ray is consistent with congestive heart failure and pneumonia. She has a long 
history of morbid obesity, chronic bronchitis, schizoaffective disorder, and cigarette smoking. She is reported to have been 
relatively stable on haloperidol, benztropine, and lithium carbonate. Her present medication regimen includes 
acetaminophen, methylprednisolone, cipro floxacin, haloperidol, benztropine, and lithium carbonate. She also requires 
intravenous lorazepam every 2 hours for agitation that caused her to fight the ventilator. Vital signs now show a spike in 
temperature to 38.9°C (102.0°F). Physical examination discloses lead pipe rigidity. Serum creatine kinase concentration 
is 846 U/L and serum lithium concentration is  0.86 mEq/L (therapeutic=0.5 –1.5). Arterial blood gas analysis while breathing 
40% oxygen is  shown:  
 
PO2 68 mm Hg  
PCO2 36 mm Hg  
pH 7.46 
O2 saturation  91% 
 
At this time it is most important to discontinue which of the following medications?

options.A: Discontinuation of benztropine is the most critical intervention.

results.response:
<answer>C</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

Explanation: The patient has developed neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), a rare but life-threatening side effect of antipsychotic medications, characterized by fever, rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic dysfunction. The patient's elevated creatine kinase level and lead pipe rigidity are indicative of muscle damage and rigidity, respectively. The patient's elevated lithium level is not the primary concern at this time. The patient's respiratory status is stable, and the arterial blood gas analysis shows a pH of 7.46, indicating alkalosis. The patient's agitation is being managed with lorazepam, which is not the primary concern. The patient's antibiotic regimen is appropriate for the treatment of pneumonia. The most critical intervention is to discontinue the antipsychotic medication, haloperidol, which is the likely cause of NMS.

results.question:
A 35 -year-old woman comes to the office because she noticed a lump in her neck 2  months ago. She has not had pain, 
difficulty swallowing, or change in her voice. She is a native of Germany and immigrated to th e United States 10  years ago. 
Medical history is significant for Hodgkin lymphoma at age 12  years treated with mantle radiation. Hypothyroidism was 
diagnosed at age 25  years. Her only routine medication is levothyroxine. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily 
for the past 10  years. She app ears well. BMI is 26  kg/m2. Temperature is 36.6°C  (97.8°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 
18/min, and blood pressure is 108/66  mm Hg. Physical examination discloses a 2.5 -cm (1-in) nodule in the right lobe  of the 
thyroid gland. There is also a 1 -cm (0.4-in) nodule in the left thyroid lobe. Results of serum thyroid function tests show a 
thyroid -stimulating hormone concentration of 0.6  μU/mL and a free thyroxine concentration of 1.7  ng/dL (N=0.7 –1.8). 
Which of the following factors in this patient is most  indicative of a poor prognosis?

options.D: Multifocal involvement of the thyroid gland
options.E: History of therapeutic radiation to the head and neck region

results.correct_answer: History of therapeutic radiation to the head and neck region
results.predicted_answer: Multifocal involvement of the thyroid gland
results.response:
<answer>D</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

* The patient has a history of Hodgkin lymphoma treated with mantle radiation, which is a significant risk factor for thyroid cancer, particularly papillary thyroid carcinoma.
* The presence of multiple nodules in the thyroid gland (multifocal involvement) is a common feature of papillary thyroid carcinoma, which is often associated with a worse prognosis.
* While tobacco use (A) is a risk factor for many cancers, it is not as strongly associated with thyroid cancer as radiation exposure.
* Elevated thyroid hormone levels (B) are not indicative of a poor prognosis in this context.
* Insufficient dietary iodine intake (C) is not a significant risk factor for thyroid cancer.
* History of therapeutic radiation to the head and neck region (E) is a significant risk factor, but multifocal involvement is a more specific and direct indicator of a poor prognosis in this patient.

behaving strangely in class and then becoming sleepy and difficult to arouse. School records indicate that the patient has no

trauma. Temperature is 37.3°C  (99.1°F), pulse is 62/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 106/62 mm Hg. Pulse 
oxim etry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96% . Pupils measure 2 mm in diameter and are minimally reactive to 
light. Cardiopulmonary examination discloses clear lungs and normal cardiac rhythm. Bowel sounds are decreased. The 
patient moans and moves all extremities to painful stimuli. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no 
abnormalities. The most likely cause of this patient's condition is abuse of which of the following medications?

options.C: An overdose of a sedating antihistamine.

results.predicted_answer: A benzodiazepine agent such as diazepam
results.response:
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

My reasoning is as follows:

The patient's symptoms, including lethargy, sleepiness, difficulty arousing, and decreased bowel sounds, are consistent with sedation. The minimally reactive pupils and decreased bowel sounds are particularly suggestive of a benzodiazepine overdose. Benzodiazepines are commonly abused substances, and their effects can include sedation, lethargy, and impaired cognitive function. The other options are less likely to cause these specific symptoms, and the patient's presentation is not consistent with an overdose of dextromethorphan, a sedating antihistamine, dextroamphetamine, or a sympathomimetic agent.

Therefore, evaluating the impact of unilateral spatial neglect on her visuospatial abilities would be crucial in determining her ability to resume work as a graphic designer.

results.question:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother for evaluation of a 6 -month history of abnormal movements that th e 
mother describes as sudden, repetitive jerks often involving his shoulders and arms. She also has witnessed multiple episodes  
of repetitive eye blinking and throat clearing. There is no aura prior to the events. Initially, the episodes occurred 
approximately once weekly, but now they seem to occur daily and are interfering with his daily activities. His school 
performance has been acce ptable, but his teacher says he is easily distracted and has difficulty sitting through class without 
fidgeting. Medical h istory is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. The patient is 133 cm (4 ft 4 in; 50th percentile) tall 
and weighs 29 kg (64 lb; 50th percentile). Vital signs are within normal limits. The patient is restless but cooperative. Mus cle 
tone, deep tendon reflexes, and gait are normal; there is no tremor. The remainder of the neurologic examination discloses no  
focal abnormalities. Durin g the examination, the patient experiences an episode of brief repetitive eye blinking; there is no 
change in consciousnes s during or after the episode. Based on these findings, this patient is most likely to develop which of 
the following?

options.A: Copper accumulation disorder affecting the liver and basal ganglia

results.response:
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

This patient's symptoms, including sudden, repetitive jerks, eye blinking, and throat clearing, are classic for Sydenham's chorea, which is a manifestation of acute rheumatic fever. While Sydenham's chorea is a self-limiting condition, it is associated with an increased risk of developing Huntington's disease later in life. Therefore, the patient is most likely to develop Huntington's chorea.

An 11 -year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents because of a 1 -day history of shortness of breath, fatigue, 
excessive thirst, and frequent urination. The parents say the onset of these symptoms coincided with an upper respiratory tra ct 
infection. Medical history is significant for type  1 diabetes mellitus. The patient's only medication is insulin. BMI is 23 kg/m2 (93rd 
percentile). Temperature is 36.0°C  (96.8°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure i s 95/6 5 mm Hg. Oxygen 
saturation is 97% on room air. The patient appears moderately dehydrated and drowsy, but she is able to speak easily when 
prompted and communicates in a lucid fashion. Her respiratory pattern is rapid and deep, and she is using accessory muscles o f 
respir ation. Results of laboratory studies are shown:  
Serum    Arterial blood gas analysis on room air  
 Acetone  45 mg/dL (N<10)   PO2 95 mm Hg  
 Glucose  693 mg/dL   PCO2 26 mm Hg  
     PH 7.16 
     Base excess  −16 mEq/L

In addition to volume expansion, which of the following is the most appropriate intervention at this time?

options.B: Intermittent intravenous bolus administration of short-acting insulin
options.C: Intermittent intravenous administration of a rapid-acting insulin preparation
options.D: Injection of a combination of rapid-onset and intermediate-duration insulin into the subcutaneous tissue
options.E: Administration of an intermediate-acting insulin preparation via the subcutaneous route

results.correct_answer: Intermittent intravenous bolus administration of short-acting insulin
results.predicted_answer: Intermittent intravenous bolus administration of short-acting insulin
results.response:
<answer>B</answer>
<belief>0.85</belief>

The patient is presenting with symptoms of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), which is a complication of type 1 diabetes mellitus. The laboratory results support this diagnosis, with a high glucose level (693 mg/dL), high acetone level (45 mg/dL), and a low pH (7.16) with a significant base deficit (-16 mEq/L). The patient's respiratory pattern is also consistent with Kussmaul breathing, which is a hallmark of DKA.

The most appropriate intervention at this time is to administer intravenous insulin to help lower the glucose level and reduce the production of ketones. Option B, intermittent intravenous bolus administration of short-acting insulin, is the most appropriate choice because it allows for rapid correction of the hyperglycemia and acidosis. This approach also allows for close monitoring of the patient's response to treatment and adjustment of the insulin dose as needed.

The other options are not the most appropriate choices at this time. Option A, intravenous sodium bicarbonate bolus, may be considered in severe cases of DKA with a pH < 6.9, but it is not the first line of treatment. Option C, intermittent intravenous administration of a rapid-acting insulin preparation, is similar to option B but may not be as effective in rapidly correcting the hyperglycemia and acidosis. Options D and E are not appropriate because they involve subcutaneous administration of insulin, which is not suitable for the acute management of DKA.

discloses an S 3 heard at the base. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. There is bilateral pitting edema  to the

options.B: Furosemide is contraindicated for this patient.

<answer>A</answer>
<belief>0.8</belief>

The patient's ECG shows a low QRS voltage, which is suggestive of a pericardial effusion. The patient's symptoms of shortness of breath and jugular venous pressure elevation also support this diagnosis. Digitalis glycosides are contraindicated in patients with pericardial effusion because they can worsen the condition by increasing the heart rate and contractility, leading to increased cardiac output and further fluid accumulation in the pericardial space. 

The other options are not correct because:

* Furosemide is a diuretic that can be used to treat the patient's edema and hypertension.
* Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor therapy is actually beneficial in patients with heart failure and hypertension, as it can help to reduce blood pressure and alleviate symptoms.
* Beta-blocker therapy is also beneficial in patients with heart failure and hypertension, as it can help to reduce the heart rate and contractility, leading to improved cardiac function.
* Aldosterone antagonist therapy is also beneficial in patients with heart failure and hypertension, as it can help to reduce fluid retention and alleviate symptoms.

g/dL. Upper endoscopy shows a nonbleeding ulcer; biopsy of the ulcer is done, and results show a polymorphic infiltrate of 
small cells wit h reactive follicles that stain positive for B lymp hocyte markers CD19 and CD20. In addition to prescribing

The patient has a nonbleeding ulcer with a polymorphic infiltrate of small cells with reactive follicles that stain positive for B lymphocyte markers CD19 and CD20. This histopathological description is suggestive of a low-grade MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue) lymphoma, specifically MALT lymphoma of the stomach. The presence of a nonbleeding ulcer and the patient's symptoms are consistent with this diagnosis.

The most appropriate initial treatment for MALT lymphoma of the stomach is a regimen of clarithromycin combined with amoxicillin, which is aimed at eradicating H. pylori infection, a known risk factor for MALT lymphoma. This approach has been shown to induce complete remission in a significant proportion of patients with MALT lymphoma.

The other options are not appropriate initial treatments for this condition. Cyclophosphamide combined with metronidazole treatment (option B) is not a standard treatment for MALT lymphoma. Irradiation (option C) may be considered in certain cases, but it is not the initial treatment of choice. Administration of rituximab (option D) may be considered in more advanced cases or in combination with other treatments. Surgical excision (option E) is not typically necessary for MALT lymphoma of the stomach.
